explanations.6: The minus sign means 'don't fail if this file doesn't exist'—it's optional.
explanations.8: This runs the shell command `id -u` right now and stores the user ID number as a variable.
explanations.9: Same as line 8, but captures the group ID instead of user ID.
explanations.13: The `?=` operator means 'use this value only if TARGET hasn't been set already'—it's a default with lower priority.
explanations.21: These are ANSI color codes wrapped in escape sequences; they make colored terminal output when combined with printf.
explanations.32: This uses `awk` to parse the Makefile itself and extract all targets with `##` comments to auto-generate help text.
explanations.38: This line creates a target that delegates to either `_bash-development` or `_bash-production` depending on the TARGET variable.
explanations.46: The `printf $(_YELLOW)` passes the color format string as the first argument, then the two strings after it fill the `%s` placeholders.
explanations.59: This is a shell for-loop inside the Makefile; the backslash continues the command across multiple lines.
explanations.60: The `$$dir` uses double-dollar because Make interprets one `$`, so you need two to get a literal `$` for the shell.
explanations.67: The colon syntax means '_build-development depends on _ensure-host-volumes being run first'.
explanations.71: The `${ARGS}` lets you pass extra Docker build flags from the command line, like `make build ARGS='--no-cache'`.
explanations.92: The `$${PWD}` uses double-dollar for the same reason as line 60—it passes a literal `${PWD}` to the shell.
explanations.97: The `|| true` part means 'if the docker stop fails, ignore the error and continue anyway'.
explanations.127: The `@-` prefix suppresses both the normal command echo AND treats errors as non-fatal.
